title: practical english usage - Part 135
content:
I don't much  like housework, and I like cooking least of all.

She always arrives when you least expect it.

Least can be used as an adverb (the opposite of most).

5 least      as  adverb For the use of the with superlatives, ►    208.

I'm least happy when I have to work at weekends.

The least expensive trips are often the most interesting.

(►   204), but with the opposite meaning.

(The) least is used before adjectives in the same way as (the) most or (the).. .est (the)   .. .est 4 (the)   least      with    adjectives:       the   opposite       of  (the)   most      or Determiners:       Quantifiers        Section  13 grammar »172     quantifying expressions: a lot, lots, a great deal, the majority, etc I've thrown out a large amount of old clothing.

Mr Lucas has spent a great deal of time in the Far East.

A great deal of and a large amount of are generally used with uncountable nouns.

These are used in similar ways to ɑ  lot of and lots of, but are more formal.

4 a  great      deal     of,   a  large      amount         of   and   a  large      number         of Don't rush. There's plenty of time.  Plenty of shops sell batteries.

uncountables and plurals. It suggests 'enough and more'.

Plenty of is usually rather informal. It is used mostly before singular 3 plenty       of Lots of us think it's time for an election.

A lot of my friends want to emigrate, (     A-lot-of-my-friends wants . ..) not Lots of patience is needed, too. (     Lets of patience are-needed,-tee;) A lot of time is needed to learn a language.

the verb is singular.

plural subject, the verb is plural; when lots of is used before a singular subject, makes a following verb singular or plural. So when a lot of is used before a nouns, and before pronouns. It is the subject, and not the form lot/lots, that and lots of. they are both used mainly before singular uncountable and plural a large number of, much  or many. There is not much difference between a lot of These are rather informal. In a more formal style, we  prefer a great deal of, 2 a  lot    of   and   lots     of not Many shops open on Sunday mornings, (     Many of shops-...) not - Plenty of shops open on Sunday mornings, (     Plenty shops...) not There's not much  meat left, (     Jlwr^s-noFnHicli-pf-menf-lefL) - There's not a lot of meat left, (xur Thwc's-not-a lot meat left.) these expressions even before nouns with no determiner. Compare: and most, but the grammar is not quite the same. In particular, of is used after Expressions of this kind have similar meanings to the determiners much,  many 1  introduction;         use   of  of a  great      deal,     the    major      ity,   e tc 172 quantifying        expressions:        a  lot,   lots, For other uses of enough, and word order with nouns and adjectives, ►    450.

The meat is enough. (The meat is not an amount.) That's enough, thank you. Enough is enough.

Half a pound of carrots will be enough.

is clear.

Enough can be used alone without a noun to refer to an amount, if the meaning 2 without      a  noun She's had enough of England; she's going back home.

I've had enough of mathematics; I'm going to give it up.

noun without a determiner.

Note the idiomatic structure I've had enough of... This can be followed by a grammar *172  quantifying expressions: a lot, lots, a great deal, the majority, etc For a couple of, ►     128.2.

He does not often speak, but when he does he says a great deal.

We should be all right for cheese - I've bought plenty.

‘ How much  money did it cost? 'A  lot.1 (     A lot of) case, of is not used.

These expressions can be used without nouns if the meaning is clear. In this 7 use   without      following       nouns lhetouHh) not They lived many miles from the town (     They-lived plenty of miles-from not It cost several pounds, (     It cost a lot of pounds.) pounds, years or miles. Other words have to be used.

These expressions are not generally used before words for units of measure, like 6 not   used    with    measurement          nouns The majority of his work is concerned with children's artistic development.
